title: MCV4U Unit 3 Lesson 1 Exit Card – Introduction to Vectors

lessonAnalysis.topic: Introduction to Geometric Vectors: Magnitude, Direction, Equality, Opposites, and Parallelism
lessonAnalysis.coreLearningGoal: Students will understand that a vector is a quantity with both magnitude and direction, distinguish vectors from scalars, represent vectors geometrically as directed line segments, and identify equal, opposite, and parallel vectors.
successCriteria: Define a vector as a quantity having both magnitude and direction.
Distinguish between vectors and scalars with examples.
Represent a vector geometrically using a directed line segment with tail and head.
Determine when two vectors are equal (same magnitude and direction).
Determine when two vectors are opposite (same magnitude, opposite direction).
Determine when two vectors are parallel (same or opposite direction).
Interpret vector direction using compass bearings (true bearing).
lessonExamplesDetected: Velocity vector with components 3 m/s east and 4 m/s north, resultant magnitude 5 m/s.
Map of GTA: distance from Toronto to Mississauga as scalar vs. vector.
Cube ABCDEFGH with side 10 cm: drawing vectors EF, GC, AB; identifying equal vectors (EF=AB), opposite vectors (FB and AE, AD and BC, EH and GF).
Boxes pushed with 5 N east: equal vectors, total force 10 N east.
True/false statements: opposite vectors are parallel (true), parallel vectors are opposite (false), equal vectors are parallel (true).
Compass bearing: true bearing measured clockwise from north.
targetProducts: Geometric representation of a vector as an arrow with tail and head.
Identification of equal vectors on a cube.
Identification of opposite vectors on a cube.
True/false reasoning about relationships between equal, opposite, and parallel vectors.
Interpretation of true bearing from a compass diagram.
inputEvidenceStudentsShouldUse: Directed line segments with labeled tail and head.
Vector arrows with given magnitudes and directions.
doNotRevealAsQuestionCondition: The final conclusion that two specific vectors are equal or opposite (e.g., 'EF equals AB') should not be given as a premise; students must deduce it.
The answer to true/false statements about vector relationships should not be provided in the stem.
The resultant magnitude or direction in a component problem should not be given if the task is to compute it.

problemAnchor: A student claims that if two vectors are parallel, they must be opposite. Is this correct? Use geometric vector concepts to justify your answer.

abt.and: Vectors have both magnitude and direction, and they can be represented geometrically as directed line segments.
abt.but: Students often confuse the relationships between equal, opposite, and parallel vectors, assuming that parallel implies opposite or that equal vectors are not parallel.
abt.therefore: We need to assess whether students can correctly identify and distinguish equal, opposite, and parallel vectors using geometric representations and true/false reasoning.

objective: Diagnose student understanding of geometric vectors: magnitude, direction, equality, opposites, and parallelism, as introduced in the first lesson of the vectors unit.

knowledgeNodes.name: Vector Definition
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Vectors are quantities that have both magnitude and direction. Magnitude is the length/size (always positive), direction indicates which way it points.

knowledgeNodes.name: Scalar vs. Vector
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Scalars measure only quantity (magnitude), e.g., distance, height. Vectors measure magnitude and direction, e.g., displacement, velocity, force.

knowledgeNodes.name: Geometric Vector Representation
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: A geometric vector is represented by a directed line segment from tail to head, not using a coordinate system. Notation: vector AB has tail at A, head at B.

knowledgeNodes.name: Equal Vectors
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Two vectors are equal if they have the same magnitude and direction. Example: on a cube, EF = AB because they have same length and point same way.

knowledgeNodes.name: Opposite Vectors
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Two vectors are opposite if they have the same magnitude but opposite direction. Example: FB and AE on a cube.

knowledgeNodes.name: Parallel Vectors
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Two vectors are parallel if their directions are either the same or opposite. Collinear vectors can be placed on the same line. Opposite vectors are always parallel, but parallel vectors are not necessarily opposite (they could be equal).

knowledgeNodes.name: True Bearing
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Direction can be given as a true bearing: angle measured clockwise from north, written with three digits (e.g., 023°).

errorTaxonomy.meaning: Confuses scalar and vector quantities (e.g., thinks distance is a vector).
Misidentifies equal vectors: thinks vectors with same magnitude but different direction are equal, or ignores direction.
Misidentifies opposite vectors: thinks vectors with different magnitudes can be opposite, or confuses opposite with parallel.
Incorrectly assumes parallel vectors must be opposite (or equal), not understanding that parallel includes both same and opposite directions.
Misinterprets geometric vector direction from tail-head order (e.g., thinks AB is same as BA).
Cannot correctly identify equal/opposite vectors on a 3D cube diagram due to spatial reasoning or misreading vertex labels.
Misunderstands true bearing: measures angle from south or counterclockwise, or does not use three-digit format.

answerLock.trueBearingDefinition: Angle measured clockwise from north, three digits.

figures.title: Two vectors on a grid: PR and RQ

stemParts.value: Which of the following is a vector quantity?

options.label: A
options.text: The height of a person (175 cm)
options.feedbackWrong: Height is a scalar; it has magnitude only.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: Temperature is a scalar; it has magnitude only.

options.label: C
options.text: A car traveling at 60 km/h east
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Velocity includes both speed (magnitude) and direction (east).

options.label: D
options.text: The mass of a book (2 kg)
options.feedbackWrong: Mass is a scalar; it has magnitude only.

options.label: A
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! The tail is the first letter, A.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: B is the head, not the tail.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: Only one point is the tail.

options.label: D
options.text: Neither, it depends on the context
options.feedbackWrong: In vector notation, the first letter is always the tail.

stemParts.value: Refer to the figure of comparison vectors. Which vector is equal to

options.label: A
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! u and v have the same magnitude and direction.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: w has the same magnitude but opposite direction.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: w is opposite, not equal.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: v is equal to u.

stemParts.value: Refer to the figure of comparison vectors. Which vector is opposite to

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: v is equal to u, not opposite.

options.label: B
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! w has the same magnitude but opposite direction to u.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: v is equal, not opposite.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: w is opposite to u.

stemParts.value: If two vectors are parallel, which of the following must be true?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: Parallel vectors can have different magnitudes.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: Parallel vectors can have opposite directions.

options.label: C
options.text: They have either the same or opposite direction.
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Parallel vectors have the same or opposite direction.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: Opposite vectors are a subset of parallel vectors, but not all parallel vectors are opposite.

stemParts.value: Refer to the compass figure. A vector points southeast. What is its true bearing?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: 045° is northeast.

options.label: B
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Southeast is 135° clockwise from north.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: 225° is southwest.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: 315° is northwest.

stemParts.value: Refer to the comparison vectors figure. Which statement about the vectors is false?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: This statement is true; u and v are equal.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: This statement is true; u is opposite to w.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: This statement is true; v is opposite to w.

options.label: D
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! u and w are opposite, not equal.

stemParts.value: A student says: "If two vectors are opposite, they are parallel. Therefore, if two vectors are parallel, they must be opposite." Is this reasoning correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, because opposite vectors are always parallel.
options.feedbackWrong: The conclusion does not follow; parallel vectors can also be equal.

options.label: B
options.text: No, because parallel vectors can also be equal.
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Parallel vectors can have the same direction (equal) or opposite direction.

options.label: C
options.text: Yes, because parallel means opposite direction.
options.feedbackWrong: Parallel includes both same and opposite directions.

options.label: D
options.text: No, because opposite vectors are not parallel.
options.feedbackWrong: Opposite vectors are indeed parallel.

stemParts.value: Explain the difference between a scalar and a vector. Give one example of each that was discussed in the lesson.

questions.rubric: Correct response: Scalar has only magnitude (e.g., distance, height, mass). Vector has magnitude and direction (e.g., displacement, velocity, force). Examples from lesson: height of a person (scalar), velocity 5 m/s east (vector).
questions.sampleStrongAnswer: A scalar is a quantity that has only magnitude, such as the height of a person (175 cm). A vector has both magnitude and direction, such as a car traveling at 60 km/h east. Scalars tell you 'how much,' while vectors tell you 'how much and which way.'

stemParts.value: Refer to the figure showing vectors
. Are these vectors equal? Justify your answer by comparing their magnitudes and directions.

questions.rubric: Correct response: No, they are not equal. Magnitude of PR = sqrt(1^2+3^2)=sqrt(10) ≈ 3.16 units, direction is down and right. Magnitude of RQ = 3 units, direction is right. Different magnitudes and directions, so not equal.
questions.sampleStrongAnswer: No, the vectors are not equal. Vector PR has a magnitude of √(1²+3²)=√10≈3.16 units and points down and to the right. Vector RQ has a magnitude of 3 units and points directly to the right. Since both the magnitudes and directions differ, the vectors are not equal.

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student cannot distinguish between scalar and vector quantities.

miniLesson.explain: A scalar is a quantity that has only magnitude (size). A vector has both magnitude and direction. Think: scalar = how much; vector = how much and which way.
miniLesson.example: Distance (scalar) vs. displacement (vector). If you walk 5 m, that's distance. If you walk 5 m north, that's displacement (a vector).
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Scalar = Scale (just a number). Vector = Arrow (number + direction).

guidedPractice.prompt: Classify: The temperature is 30°C. Scalar or vector?
Classify: A force of 10 N downward. Scalar or vector?
A car's speedometer reads 80 km/h. Is this a scalar or vector? What additional information would make it a vector?
guidedPractice.answer: Scalar (speed). To make it a vector (velocity), add direction, e.g., 80 km/h north.

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student incorrectly identifies equal vectors, often ignoring direction or magnitude.

miniLesson.explain: Two vectors are equal if they have BOTH the same magnitude (length) AND the same direction. If either differs, they are not equal.
miniLesson.example: In the comparison vectors figure, u and v have same length and both point right, so they are equal. u and w have same length but opposite directions, so they are not equal.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Equal vectors = Same size, Same way (SS).

guidedPractice.prompt: Vector u has magnitude 5 and points east. Vector v has magnitude 5 and points east. Are they equal?
Vector a has magnitude 3 and points north. Vector b has magnitude 3 and points south. Are they equal?
In the comparison vectors figure, is vector v equal to vector w? Explain.
guidedPractice.answer: No, v points right, w points left. They have opposite directions, so they are not equal.

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student misidentifies opposite vectors, confusing them with equal or unrelated vectors.

miniLesson.explain: Two vectors are opposite if they have the SAME magnitude but OPPOSITE direction. They are parallel but point opposite ways.
miniLesson.example: In the comparison vectors figure, u and w have the same length but u points right, w points left. So they are opposite.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Opposite vectors = Same size, Opposite way (SO).

guidedPractice.prompt: Vector u points east. What direction does its opposite vector point?
Vector a has magnitude 4 and points north. Vector b has magnitude 4 and points south. Are they opposite?
In the comparison vectors figure, is vector v opposite to vector w? Explain.
guidedPractice.answer: Yes, v points right, w points left, and they have the same length. So they are opposite.

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student thinks parallel vectors must be opposite, or does not understand that parallel includes both same and opposite directions.

miniLesson.explain: Parallel vectors have the same or opposite direction. They lie on the same line or parallel lines. Opposite vectors are a subset of parallel vectors. Equal vectors are also parallel (same direction).
miniLesson.example: If vector u points east and vector v points east, they are parallel (and equal if same magnitude). If vector w points west, it is parallel to u (opposite direction). So parallel does NOT imply opposite; it could be same direction.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Parallel = Same line or parallel lines, direction can be same or opposite.

guidedPractice.prompt: True or False: All opposite vectors are parallel.
True or False: All parallel vectors are opposite.
guidedPractice.answer: False. They could be in the same direction.

guidedPractice.prompt: If vector a is parallel to vector b, and vector b is parallel to vector c, is vector a always parallel to vector c? Explain.
guidedPractice.answer: Yes, because parallelism is transitive. If a is parallel to b (same or opposite direction), and b is parallel to c, then a is parallel to c (direction either same or opposite to c).

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student misinterprets the tail-head order of a geometric vector.

miniLesson.explain: In vector AB, the first letter is the tail (starting point), the second letter is the head (ending point). The vector points from A to B.
miniLesson.example: Vector PQ: tail at P, head at Q. Vector QP: tail at Q, head at P. They are opposite vectors.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: First letter = Foot (tail), Second letter = Head.

guidedPractice.prompt: In vector XY, which point is the head?
If vector AB points east, what direction does vector BA point?
On a grid, vector EF and vector FE: are they equal or opposite?
guidedPractice.answer: Opposite. EF goes from E to F, FE from F to E.

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student struggles to identify equal/opposite vectors on a diagram, possibly due to spatial reasoning or misreading labels.

miniLesson.explain: On a diagram, vectors along parallel lines with the same direction are equal if they have the same length. Use the labels to determine direction: vector from tail to head.
miniLesson.example: In the comparison vectors figure, u and v are equal because they have same length and both point right. u and w are opposite because they have same length but opposite directions.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Equal vectors = parallel, same length, same direction. Opposite vectors = parallel, same length, opposite direction.

guidedPractice.prompt: In the comparison vectors figure, are u and v equal?
In the comparison vectors figure, are u and w opposite?
In the comparison vectors figure, is v equal to w? Explain.
guidedPractice.answer: No, v points right, w points left. They are opposite, not equal.

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student misunderstands true bearing: measures from wrong reference or wrong direction.

miniLesson.explain: True bearing is the angle measured clockwise from true north, always written with three digits (e.g., 045°). North = 000°, East = 090°, South = 180°, West = 270°.
miniLesson.example: Southeast is exactly halfway between south (180°) and east (090°), so bearing = 135°.

guidedPractice.prompt: What is the true bearing of east?
A vector points southwest. What is its true bearing?
A ship sails on a bearing of 300°. In which direction is it traveling?
guidedPractice.answer: Northwest (between west 270° and north 360°/000°, so 300° is 30° west of north, i.e., northwest).

questions.stem: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
Give an example of a vector quantity and explain why it is a vector.
questions.rubric: Must include magnitude and direction. Example: Velocity (e.g., 50 km/h north) because it has speed (magnitude) and direction.

questions.stem: Two vectors are equal if they have:
Vector u has magnitude 5 and points north. Vector v has magnitude 5 and points north. Are they equal? Justify.
Two vectors are opposite if they have:
Vector a points east. Describe its opposite vector.
If two vectors are parallel, they:

options.label: D
options.text: Have either the same or opposite direction

questions.stem: Can two vectors be parallel but not opposite? Explain with an example.
questions.rubric: Yes, if they have the same direction. Example: two vectors both pointing east.

questions.stem: In vector CD, the head is at:
If vector PQ points north, what direction does vector QP point?
In the comparison vectors figure, which vectors are equal?
In the comparison vectors figure, name a pair of opposite vectors.
questions.rubric: u and w, or v and w.

questions.stem: The true bearing of west is:
A vector points northeast. What is its true bearing?

teacherEscalation.condition: Student scores 0-4 on diagnostic card after reteach and retest cycles.
teacherEscalation.message: Student may need one-on-one instruction focusing on the fundamental definition of vectors, geometric representation, and the relationships between equal, opposite, and parallel vectors. Use physical manipulatives (e.g., arrows on a whiteboard, 3D cube model) to reinforce spatial reasoning. Consider additional practice with compass bearings and vector notation.
